front: Work: this sequence of three Petrarchan sonnets was inspired by the 1909 suicide of railway worker Romelio Ureta
Work: 'From the frozen niche where men placed you, I will lower you to the humble, sunny earth' opens this poem sequence
Work: the second of these poems promises to 'leave singing my beautiful vengeance' and insists 'we must dream on the same pillow'
Work: the third of these poems confronts God ('You are going to judge me') and describes 'wicked hands' and 'the rosy boat of his life stopped'
Work: this poem sequence won the Chilean 'Floral Games' literary contest in 1914
Work: 'You shall love beauty, which is the shadow of God over the Universe' opens this ten-stanza instructional poem
Work: this poem declares 'Beauty shall not be an opiate but a strong wine' and 'there is no godless art'
Work: this Chilean poet's debut collection contains the Sonnets of Death
Work: this collection mourns the death of the poet's mother; opens with eight poems following her through 'black mountains' and 'countries the color of a bruise'
Work: this collection's title refers to felling trees; its 'Matter' section contains poems about bread, salt, and water
Work: in this poem, a cripple and invalid are told to dance with their hearts; it ends 'the heart of him who joins us not / Is turned to dust, to dust'
Author: this Chilean poet took her pen name from the Occitan poet Frederic Mistral, who wrote Mireio and won the 1904 Nobel
Author: Langston Hughes translated this Chilean poet's works (1957), grouping them as 'Cradle Songs' and 'Poems for Mothers'
Author: this poet's lover and translator Doris Dana wrote the children's fable The Elephant and His Secret
Author: this poet identified as 'Mestiza de Vasco' due to mixed Amerindian-Basque heritage
Author: this poet's nephew and adopted son Yin-Yin committed suicide, influencing her collection Lagar
Author: this poet wrote about children: 'our worst crime is abandoning our children / To him we cannot answer Tomorrow, his name is today'
